author: lukaszrozmej
content: <@427491045308235776> how zkVM's plan to tackle increased computation from rising gas and BAL's? Yeah I know repricing, but it won't cover everything. Yeah I know adding more GPU's, but what about initial block execution? How long does it take now? How long will it take with BAL's? Will we leverage BAL's in a way to split block execution horizontally to (per transaction/batch of transactions)?

author: jsign
content:
<@628018527470616606>, sharing my view. Reg BALs, if you refer to the extra work they entail, I would say it is indirectly captured in repricing measurements, since the marginal cost of opcodes that involve BAL work is indirectly captured (I would say mostly in encoding and hashing marginal cost rather than state tracker work to know what to inlcude).

Reg initial block execution (I think you refer to the execution in the RISCV emulator (CPU only) to generate the trace to prove later (with GPUs)), yes this is a relevant question. The emulation time varies a lot with zkVMs, but for Zisk IIRC we saw around 2s in a machine we rented, but prob we want to re-measure again whenever we have the new proving cluster.

And yes, we want to experiment with using BALs as an enshrined protocol datastructure to split block proving into multiple parts (i.e. subblock proving). The idea is as you say to split the execution in parallel parts that later you can aggregate by checking receipts, gas used, partial bals, etc. So it would be the same logic of parallelization you use in re-execution style, but in proving it would be different guest programs execution where their individual proofs are aggregated in a parent guest program that "glues" all together.  This allows to parallelize the execution part by paying some extra potential overhead depending on how efficient is proof verification aggregation for zkVMs.

author: jsign
content: Reg gas limit increases in general, I would say all depends on how fast re-execution throughput (with bals, G/H repricings, or optimizations) compares with proving throughput with time. I think here the opinion varies out there on projections, so the best way is benchmark and track how things look like and see as zkVMs plan to continue doing their optimizations. Might also depend what "increasing gas limit" means reg the new repricings since 1 gas in the future won't look the same as 1 gas today (due to repricings), but the question is still valid.

author: lukaszrozmej
content: well we have 60mgas blocks, thing 600mgas blocks for example

author: jsign
content:
Yes, at that point the main question is which is going to be the bottleneck.
For 600Mgas seems quite clear that the RISCV emulation would definitely be a bottleneck since it is single-threaded, so at that point I would assume we already must do sub-block proving, which basically splits the RISCV emulation of the whole block in as many parts we want.
Under that assumption:
- The RISCV emulation is parallelized, so we would need to see the tradeoffs between more partitions and aggregation of those individual parts proofs verification to have a single proof.
- Proving the execution trace is already fully parallelized today with GPUs.
So that means that most stages of the pipeline are parallelizable -- so the conclusion depends on zkVM optimizations or proving system efficiency gains compared to our target hardware (i.e. CapEx/Opex for proving machines).

I also suspect that at that 600Mgas point, we'll need to do more things in the execution witness generation which happens before zkVM stuff during block building. So block building for 600Mgas prob will take some time, and we might need to start considering streaming the execution witness while the block execution still is happening to maybe pipeline the process (i.e. today the mental model is "block building and execution witness generation" and then "zkVM prove the block" -- but that sounds quite complex since might need other kind of engineAPI changes or similar)
